descriptor.label: CoreLab Analytics is
descriptor.body: An interactive platform that converts raw research data into immediate visual insights— replacing static papers with a dynamic dashboard. The companion study is a quantitative correlational investigation of how computer laboratory usage predicts academic performance change among BSIT students at STI College Malolos.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.lead: The problem, the variables, the questions, and the conceptual ground the study is built on.

beats.label: Context
beats.body: IT education is fundamentally practical. Lecture material alone is insufficient— students in BSIT programs require the computer laboratory to run code, configure networks, and develop applied technical skills. Local and international studies confirm that ICT integration and guided laboratory access are tied to better student outcomes.

beats.label: The Gap
beats.body: Existing research focuses on general, end-of-term results. What is critically underexamined is performance shift within a single academic term— specifically, whether laboratory engagement during the Preliminary period corresponds to measurable academic improvement by the Midterm period. This study addresses that gap at STI College Malolos.

sections.title: The Variables

cards.title: Frequency
cards.definition: Weekly laboratory hours attended, including both scheduled class time and voluntary extra lab sessions.
cards.measurement: Self-reported numeric hours per week (ratio scale). Winsorized: one outlier capped at 10 hrs.

cards.title: Intensity
cards.definition: Composite Behavioral Engagement Score derived from five Likert-scale items measuring active technical behaviors (coding, configuration, debugging), with passive items reverse-coded.
cards.measurement: Aggregated score across Q8.1–Q8.5; Q8.2 and Q8.4 are reverse-coded. Scale: 5–25.

cards.title: Performance Change Score
cards.definition: The measurable shift in academic performance between grading periods— computed as Midterm Grade minus Preliminary Grade.
cards.measurement: Numerical grade difference per respondent. Positive = improvement.

sections.context: 86 BSIT students at STI College Malolos across five core subjects: Computer Programming 1 and 2, Event-Driven Programming, Systems Administration and Maintenance, and Introduction to Computing.

sections.title: Behavioral Indicators of Intensity
sections.lead: The Composite Behavioral Score is built from five Likert-scaled items (1 = Strongly Disagree, 5 = Strongly Agree). Passive or non-technical behaviors are reverse-coded so that higher composite values consistently represent deeper technical engagement.

items.text: I actively wrote or modified code during my laboratory sessions.
Most of my lab time was spent reviewing theoretical concepts rather than doing hands-on tasks.
I executed technical configurations (networks, systems, environments) on lab machines.
I often used lab time for non-academic activities (browsing, chatting, gaming).
I spent time debugging or troubleshooting technical problems during the session.

sections.scale: Composite range = 5–25. Internal consistency: Cronbach's α = 0.70 (acceptable).

sections.title: The Research Questions
sections.central: Does computer laboratory usage— measured through Frequency (weekly laboratory hours) and Intensity (composite behavioral engagement score)— correspond to changes in academic performance between the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods in selected core BSIT subjects at STI College Malolos?
subs: What is the relationship between laboratory usage and Preliminary academic performance?
What is the relationship between laboratory usage and Midterm academic performance?
Is increased laboratory usage associated with positive performance change between the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods?

sections.title: Hypotheses
sections.lead: Two competing statements frame the inferential test. Significance is judged at α = 0.05— if the combined regression's p-value falls below this threshold, the null is rejected.

hypotheses.label: H₁ (Alternative)
hypotheses.text: Higher laboratory usage is associated with a positive change in academic performance between the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods.

hypotheses.label: H₀₁ (Null)
hypotheses.text: Laboratory usage is not associated with a significant change in academic performance between the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods.

sections.title: Significance of the Study
sections.generalObjective: To examine the relationship between computer laboratory usage and changes in academic performance across the Preliminary and Midterm grading periods in selected core BSIT subjects at STI College Malolos.

objectives.text: Measure the Frequency and Intensity of computer laboratory usage among BSIT students.
Determine the relationship between laboratory usage and Preliminary academic performance.
Determine the relationship between laboratory usage and Midterm academic performance.
Assess whether increased laboratory usage corresponds to positive performance change, using the computed Performance Change Score.
Develop a web-based tool for survey distribution, data visualization, and dissemination of findings.

cards.title: Students
cards.body: Understanding how laboratory time translates to measurable academic progress, enabling more intentional use of available hours within the term.

cards.title: Faculty and Instructors
cards.body: Insight into when and how laboratory activities contribute most to performance, informing the design and pacing of laboratory exercises across the term.

cards.title: Administrators and Policymakers
cards.body: Empirical basis for scheduling decisions, laboratory access equity policies, and resource allocation at the institutional level.

cards.title: Future Researchers
cards.body: A methodological foundation for examining intra-term performance shifts in technical education contexts, with identified variables and gaps for extension.

sections.title: Synthesis of the Literature
sections.subtitle: Three converging themes from the reviewed studies
sections.body: Three converging themes emerge from the reviewed literature: structured technology use improves student focus and engagement; equitable access to ICT resources determines whether students can convert instruction into applicable skills; and sustained laboratory practice builds technical competency over time. Together, these studies confirm that laboratory-based learning supports academic outcomes— but none directly examines how Frequency and Intensity of usage correlate with intra-term performance change. This study addresses that gap.

sections.title: Structured Technology Use and Academic Performance
sections.subtitle: Limniou (2021); Canoy et al. (2023)
sections.body: Guided, purposeful use of technology in the laboratory improves student focus and performance outcomes. Limniou (2021) found that students using devices under structured academic intent outperformed those with unrestricted access. Canoy et al. (2023) demonstrated beneficial effects of supervised lab sessions while identifying practical barriers: inadequate equipment, scheduling conflicts, and insufficient instructor preparation. The consistent finding: structure is as important as access.
sections.gap: Limited evidence exists on how structured lab use affects short-term, intra-term academic performance specifically in BSIT programs.

sections.title: Access to ICT Resources and Learning Outcomes
sections.subtitle: Mohamed (2025); Siega (2025); Escubido et al. (2025)
sections.body: Access to ICT resources is a prerequisite for skill development. Mohamed (2025) found that availability significantly impacts performance and recommended extending ICT access beyond laboratories. Siega (2025) confirmed a positive relationship between computer technology use and academic outcomes. Escubido et al. (2025) found that effective ICT integration in instruction improves performance, while persistent resource limitations continue to constrain student progress.
sections.gap: These studies measure access as a binary condition. None examines duration and behavioral depth of usage as a predictor of performance change across grading periods.

sections.title: Digital Skills Development Through Laboratory Practice
sections.subtitle: Vahid et al. (2023); Cadiz-Gabejan & Takenaka (2021)
sections.body: Repeated hands-on laboratory engagement is the mechanism through which BSIT students develop technical competency. Vahid et al. (2023) found that students who consistently engaged in coding activities achieved higher grades, with consistent engagement being the operative variable. Cadiz-Gabejan and Takenaka (2021) found that initial computer literacy amplifies the benefit students derive from lab work.
sections.gap: Neither study quantifies behavioral intensity or separates it from time-based frequency as a distinct predictor.

sections.title: Theoretical Framework Overview
sections.subtitle: Three theories converge to predict the study's outcomes
sections.body: Three theories collectively explain how laboratory usage may influence academic performance. Cognitive Load Theory accounts for how structured access reduces mental inefficiency. Constructivist Learning Theory explains how active engagement builds knowledge. Experiential Learning Theory connects repeated practice to measurable skill development. Together, they predict that consistent and intentional laboratory usage— high in both Frequency and Intensity— creates the conditions for a positive Performance Change Score.

sections.title: Cognitive Load Theory
sections.subtitle: Sweller (1988)
sections.body: Students have limited mental capacity at any given time. Laboratory environments that are well-managed and consistently accessible reduce extraneous cognitive load— the effort spent on logistical friction— and redirect that capacity toward germane load, where actual learning occurs. High Frequency creates a stable, low-friction environment. High Intensity ensures that mental effort within that environment is directed at technical skill-building rather than passive presence.

sections.title: Constructivist Learning Theory
sections.subtitle: Piaget (1972) & Vygotsky (1978)
sections.body: Knowledge is constructed through active experience and social interaction. Piaget's concept of schema formation through trial and correction, and Vygotsky's Zone of Proximal Development, both find direct expression in the computer laboratory. Increased laboratory hours expand the opportunity for knowledge construction. High behavioral engagement— active coding, troubleshooting, and configuration— is the mechanism through which that construction occurs.

sections.title: Experiential Learning Theory
sections.subtitle: Kolb (1984)
sections.body: Learning proceeds through a four-stage cycle: concrete experience → reflective observation → abstract conceptualization → active experimentation. The laboratory provides the site for concrete experience. Frequency determines how often the cycle is entered. Intensity determines how fully it is engaged. Repeated consistently across a term, this cycle produces the measurable improvement captured by the Performance Change Score.

sections.title: References
sections.lead: Works cited across the Review of Related Literature and the Theoretical Framework. Click any entry to open the source.

sections.title: Methods
sections.lead: How the study was scoped, designed, sampled, collected, and analyzed.

sections.title: Scope and Delimitation
sections.lead: What the study covers— and the boundaries that keep the findings interpretable.
leftItems: BSIT students at STI College Malolos with paired Preliminary + Midterm grades
Selected core IT subjects requiring computer laboratory use
Laboratory usage measured as Frequency (weekly hours) and Intensity (composite behavioral score)
Performance Change Score computed as Midterm − Preliminary
rightItems: Objective attendance logs / registrar grade records (study uses self-report)
External factors: tutoring, home internet, study habits, attendance in non-lab classes

sections.title: Quantitative Correlational Design
sections.lead: The study examined naturally occurring data— no variables were manipulated. Correlation answered the relationship questions; regression tested whether usage metrics predict performance change.

blocks.heading: Why Correlational
blocks.body: The aim was to determine the nature, degree, and direction of the statistical relationship between laboratory usage and academic performance— not to establish cause.

blocks.heading: Pearson r
blocks.body: Used to test the strength and direction of association between Frequency / Intensity and grades at each grading period.

blocks.heading: Linear Regression
blocks.body: Used to test whether the usage metrics significantly predict the Performance Change Score between Preliminary and Midterm.

blocks.heading: Limitation
blocks.body: Correlational design cannot establish causation— only that the variables move together within the sample.

sections.title: Statistical Methods Mapped to Research Questions
sections.lead: Each research question is answered by a specific statistical procedure executed on the cleaned dataset (N = 86, α = 0.05).
sections.note: All tests executed in Google Sheets via the XL Miner Analysis ToolPak.

sections.title: Participants and Sampling
sections.lead: Population of BSIT students was filtered, then sized using Slovin's formula at a 90% confidence level and 10% margin of error.

steps.label: Initial population
steps.note: Total BSIT enrollees at STI College Malolos

formula.caption: n = 406 / (1 + 406 × 0.10²) = 406 / 5.06 ≈ 80.24, rounded up to 81. The team collected 86 valid responses, exceeding the minimum.

sections.title: Inclusion Criteria
sections.lead: A respondent's data was retained only when all five conditions were satisfied. Otherwise the response was dropped from analysis.
rows: Enrolled BSIT student at STI College Malolos
Defines the target population of the study
Enrolled in / completed a selected core IT subject with a lab component
Aligns the response with the variables under study
Able to report attended laboratory hours per week
Able to self-evaluate technical engagement using the survey indicators
Required to compute the Intensity composite
Has complete paired Preliminary + Midterm grades for the subject
Required to compute the Performance Change Score

sections.title: Instrument and Distribution
sections.lead: A digitally distributed structured survey gathered all data points for the analysis.

blocks.heading: Instrument Design
blocks.body: Structured survey covering: subject enrolled, weekly laboratory hours (Frequency), five Likert items (Intensity), and paired Preliminary + Midterm grades. Items were reviewed for clarity prior to release.

blocks.heading: Approval
blocks.body: Institutional approval secured before any distribution. Survey items vetted by faculty advisers.

blocks.heading: Distribution Channels
blocks.body: Class group chats, direct text messages, and printed QR codes— chosen to reach BSIT students efficiently while keeping responses organized in a single online sheet.

blocks.heading: Window
blocks.body: Open for one collection cycle until the Slovin minimum was met; closed at 86 valid, complete responses.

sections.title: Ethical Safeguards
sections.lead: Each participant's involvement was protected by four explicit safeguards stated on the survey's first page.
rows: Participants read the study's purpose, scope, and use of responses before consenting to proceed.
Right to skip items or withdraw at any point with no academic consequence.
No personally identifying information collected; responses linked only to subject and grades.
Raw responses accessible only to the research team; results analyzed and reported in aggregate.

sections.title: Data Preparation Steps
sections.lead: Before analysis, the raw responses passed through a sequence of cleaning and computation steps applied uniformly across the dataset.
rows: Drop incomplete responses (missing grades or Likert items)
Compute Performance Change Score = Midterm − Preliminary
Winsorize Frequency outlier (one report of 20 hrs → 10 hrs)
Verify Cronbach's α on the Intensity composite

sections.title: Data Analysis Plan
sections.lead: Analysis ran in Google Sheets with the XL Miner Analysis ToolPak. Significance threshold α = 0.05 throughout.

blocks.heading: Descriptive Statistics
blocks.body: Mean, SD, min, max for weekly lab hours, Composite Behavioral Score, Preliminary Grade, Midterm Grade, and Performance Change Score.

blocks.heading: Reliability— Cronbach's α
blocks.body: Computed across the 5 Likert items of the Intensity metric to confirm internal consistency before using the composite score in inferential analysis.

blocks.heading: Pearson r— 4 Pairings
blocks.body: Frequency × Prelim, Intensity × Prelim, Frequency × Midterm, Intensity × Midterm— separating the two usage dimensions across both grading periods.

blocks.heading: Linear Regression
blocks.body: Simple regression for each metric, plus multiple regression with both predictors, to test whether Frequency and / or Intensity significantly predict the Performance Change Score.

sections.title: Statistical Tests Plan
sections.lead: The four planned tests, the variables they consume, and the decision rule applied to each.
rows: Reported as mean, SD, min, max
Confirm internal consistency of the composite
Test predictive power of usage on change
sections.note: Models: A = Frequency only · B = Intensity only · C = Both predictors combined.

sections.title: How to Read the Output · Part 1
sections.lead: Each test produces a specific kind of number. These are the two foundational checks before any inference.
leftItems: Mean (central tendency), SD (spread), min/max (range) for every numeric variable.
Reading: a small SD means responses cluster tightly around the mean; a large SD signals high variability.
Comparing means across periods (Prelim vs Midterm) gives the first hint of a performance shift before any inferential test.
Output is reported as a descriptive table — the same table format the Tool generates.
rightItems: A single coefficient between 0 and 1 estimating internal consistency of a multi-item scale.
Reading: α ≥ 0.90 = excellent · 0.80–0.89 = good · 0.70–0.79 = acceptable · < 0.70 = questionable.
If α < 0.70, the five Intensity items are not behaving as one scale and the composite cannot be trusted in regression.
In this study α = 0.70 — the composite is retained for use as the Intensity predictor.

sections.title: How to Read the Output · Part 2
sections.lead: These two inferential tests answer the research questions and decide the hypothesis.
leftItems: Output: r ∈ [−1, +1] and a p-value, computed for each of the four Frequency/Intensity × Prelim/Midterm pairings.
Reading the sign: positive r = variables move together; negative r = they move in opposite directions.
Reading the magnitude: |r| ≈ .10 weak · .30 moderate · .50+ strong (Cohen's conventions).
Reading the p-value: p < .05 means the relationship is unlikely to be due to chance — reject the null for that pairing.
rightItems: Output: R² (variance explained), F (overall model fit), β coefficients (per-predictor effect), and a p-value for each.
R² interpretation: R² = 0.25 means usage explains 25% of variation in performance change — the rest is unexplained.
β interpretation: a β of +1.4 for Frequency means each additional weekly lab hour predicts a +1.4 grade-point change, holding Intensity constant.
Decision: reject H₀₁ if the combined model's p < 0.05 — meaning Frequency and/or Intensity meaningfully predict change.
